sections.heading: Cluster Class
sections.body:
`M40+` clusters offer multiple options, including:

- **Low CPU**

- **General**

- **Local NVMe SSD**

All production environments can use the **General** option.

Select the **Class** box with your preferred speed.
Changes to cluster class affect [cluster tier](instance-size-costs).

sections.heading: Low CPU Class
sections.body:
Generally, the **Low CPU** option offers a lower-cost
alternative for applications that require more memory and fewer CPUs.
This option includes half the vCPUs of an instance with the
**General** option of the same tier. For example, a
**General** `M40` instance includes 4 vCPUs, while a
**Low CPU** `M40` instance includes 2 vCPUs. Depending on the
cluster tier, this option may also include fewer max connections. To
learn more, see [Connection Limits and Cluster Tier](connection-limits).

> **Note:** Atlas provisions storage for **Low CPU** and **General** clusters in gibibytes (GiB), despite the UI showing **GB**.

sections.heading: NVMe Storage
sections.body:
You can select the **Local NVMe SSD** storage option for some
dedicated clusters that run on AWS (Amazon Web Services) or Azure (Microsoft Azure). Locally attached
ephemeral [NVMe storage volumes](nvme-storage) offer the highest level of speed and
performance. Atlas provisions storage for NVMe clusters in gigabytes (GB).

[A File Copy Based Initial Sync](manual/core/replica-set-sync) will always be used to
sync all of the nodes of an NVME cluster whenever an initial sync is required.

> **Note:** Atlas doesn't support NVMe (non-volatile memory express) clusters on Google Cloud.

sections.heading: Storage Capacity
sections.body:
To change the server data volume size, do one of the following tasks:

- Move the slider until the text box displays your preferred disk size.

- Specify the exact disk size in the text box.

For Azure (Microsoft Azure)-specific instructions, see [Extend Storage Capacity and IOPS on Azure](change-storage-capacity-azure).

Changes to storage capacity affect [cluster tier](instance-size-costs).

> **Note:** MongoDB uses a small portion of your specified storage capacity for
> buffer files, journal files, and log files to ensure proper
> cluster operation. In no-overwrite storage engines like
> the [WiredTiger storage engine](manual/core/wiredtiger),
> you should expect to use approximately 20% more disk space than your
> compressed data occupies.

sections.heading: Oplog Size Behavior
sections.body:
Atlas manages the cluster's [oplog size](manual/core/replica-set-oplog)
and its entries differently, depending on whether [storage auto-scaling](howitworks-scale-cluster-storage)
is enabled for the cluster.

- Atlas enables cluster storage auto-scaling by default. If you
  choose to use storage auto-scaling, Atlas manages the oplog entries
  based on the [minimum oplog retention window (oplogMinRetentionHours)](manual/core/replica-set-oplog)
  setting. The oplog entries are time-stamped. The oplog window is the
  time difference between the newest and the oldest timestamps in the oplog.

  By default, Atlas sets the minimum oplog retention window to 24 hours.
  This means that, unless you set the minimum oplog retention window to
  a particular custom value, the mongod for the Atlas cluster retains all
  oplog entries for at least 24 hours and until the oplog reaches the maximum
  size that MongoDB best practices permit.

  You can [minimum retention window for Oplog entries](set-oplog-min-window)
  to a particular value in the Atlas UI. For storage auto-scaling to
  complete successfully, Atlas requires the [minimum oplog retention window (oplogMinRetentionHours)](manual/core/replica-set-oplog) up to (60 seconds) \* (GB of disk space configured).

  If the cluster's storage capacity decreases, Atlas
  automatically scales the oplog size down to ensure it fits in the
  decreased storage size.

- You can opt out of cluster storage auto-scaling by un-checking the
  Storage Scaling checkbox in the Auto-scale
  section. If you opt out of storage auto-scaling, Atlas manages
  the oplog size as follows:

  - If you don't specify the oplog size in the UI, Atlas sets the oplog
    size to:

    - 5% of the disk size when you create a cluster, for
      General and Low-CPU clusters

    - 10% of the disk size for clusters with [NVMe storage volumes](nvme-storage).

    Atlas automatically changes the oplog size if you change the storage size
    only if you have previously manually set a custom oplog size for your cluster.
    If you have never manually set the oplog size, Atlas does not automatically
    scale the oplog size when you change the storage size.

  - You may choose to scale up the oplog size when you scale up the
    cluster's storage. In this case, manually [Set Oplog Size](set-oplog-size)
    to a particular value when you create a cluster. Only after you have
    manually set a custom oplog size will Atlas automatically scale the oplog
    size when you increase the cluster's storage, as follows:

    - For General and Low-CPU clusters, the
      oplog size scales up to remain at 5% of the storage capacity, not to
      exceed a certain maximum determined according to MongoDB best practices.

    - For clusters with NVMe storage, the oplog size scales up to
      remain at 10% of the storage capacity, not to exceed a certain
      maximum determined according to MongoDB best practices.

  - If you scale down the cluster's storage, Atlas uses the previous oplog-to-disk
    ratio to scale down the oplog proportionately. For example, if you scale
    from 100 GB to 50 GB with an oplog of 25 GB (ratio of 0.25), the new oplog size
    would also have a ratio of 0.25, which would make it 12.5 GB. The only exception is if
    the new oplog size is less than 5% of the storage capacity (or less than 10% for NVMe storage). In that case, Atlas uses the higher value for the oplog size, which would be 5% of the storage capacity (10% for NVMe storage).

sections.heading: Change Storage Capacity or IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on AWS (Amazon Web Services)
sections.body:
Atlas handles changes differently based on whether you want to
increase or decrease storage capacity or storage throughput
(IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second)) on AWS (Amazon Web Services).

sections.heading: Increase Capacity or Throughput
sections.body:
If previous changes are completed, AWS (Amazon Web Services) usually takes about six hours to
increase storage capacity or IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second). However, this time frame can vary
depending on the data size stored on disk. To learn more, see the
[AWS documentation](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/monitoring-volume-modifications.html#).
Atlas supports more changes within the time frame for volume modification
changes.

AWS (Amazon Web Services) supports extended storage with the following limitations:

- `M40`: up to 4TB

- `M50`/`M60`: up to 8TB

- `M80+`: up to 14TB

> **Note:** Extended storage is available only for `M40+` clusters. To change your
> cluster's tier, see [Modify a Cluster](modify-cluster).

If you enable extended storage, this slows down initial syncs and cross-project
snapshot restores, and can reduce high availability. Enabling extended
storage is a temporary solution for clusters that might require additional
storage capacity in the future. We recommend that you enable [sharding](manual/sharding)
for long-term expanded storage capacity.

How Atlas handles additional changes within the time frame for volume
modification changes depends on the size of your hosts' data volumes and
the time elapsed since the volume was last modified. AWS (Amazon Web Services) has a cooldown
period of 6 hours, or 6 hours per TB of volume size, whichever is greater.

- For the first change, Atlas modifies data volumes in place without
  downtime when increasing disk size.

- For subsequent disk size increases:

  | Data Volume Size | Time Since Last Storage Change                                 | Action Atlas Takes                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
  | ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | Less than 1TB    | Less than 6 hours                                              | Atlas provisions new volumes and syncs the data from the old volumes. If Atlas provisions new volumes, you can access your cluster. You can't access nodes that AWS (Amazon Web Services) modifies until AWS (Amazon Web Services) attaches the new volume.                                                                                                         |
  | Less than 1TB    | 6 hours or more                                                | Atlas waits until the cooldown period expires, then modifies the hosts' data volumes in place without downtime.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
  | More than 1TB    | Less than volume modification cooldown period (6 hours per TB) | Atlas provisions new volumes and syncs the data from the old volumes. If Atlas provisions new volumes, you can access your cluster. You can't access nodes that AWS (Amazon Web Services) modifies until AWS (Amazon Web Services) attaches the new volume.                                                                                                         |
  | More than 1TB    | Volume modification cooldown period or more (6 hours per TB)   | Atlas waits until the cooldown period expires, then modifies the hosts' data volumes in place without downtime. This takes less time than provisioning new volumes and syncing data from the old to the new volumes. If you modify the disk size within 4 hours of a previous disk modification, Atlas prioritizes compute upscaling first, then disk modification. |

Before you apply your storage capacity or IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) increases, the
**Review Changes** page describes how Atlas approaches the
increase.

The **Clusters** page displays a banner if Atlas
waits until a six-hour window closes before modifying your cluster's
storage capacity or IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second):

- Some EBS volume changes are delayed due to AWS (Amazon Web Services) restrictions on volume
  increases while previous disk change is still optimizing.

These behaviors apply when Atlas changes capacity during
[Cluster Auto-Scaling](cluster-autoscaling).

To learn more about AWS (Amazon Web Services)'s limitations, see the
[AWS documentation](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/modify-volume-requirements.html#elastic-volumes-limitations).

sections.heading: Decrease Capacity or Throughput
sections.body:
- AWS (Amazon Web Services) doesn't allow you to reduce storage *capacity* in place.

  Atlas *can* reduce storage capacity by first provisioning new
  volumes and then syncing data from the old to the new volumes with an
  in-place rsync process. This works around the AWS (Amazon Web Services) limitation, but
  there is downtime on each node while this process is in progress.

- AWS (Amazon Web Services) *does* allow you to reduce IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) without migrating data.

  For disk size decreases, Atlas always provisions new volumes and
  syncs data from the old to the new volumes. This process requires downtime
  on each node during the rsync operation, regardless of the volume
  modification cooldown period.

sections.heading: Change Storage Capacity and IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on Azure (Microsoft Azure)
sections.body:
For **Low CPU** or **General** clusters deployed on Azure (Microsoft Azure),
you can extend IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) and the data volumes' storage capacity in the Atlas UI.

If you increase disk storage for **Low CPU** or **General**
clusters, you can:

- Receive more IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on clusters in the same cluster tier.

- Use these clusters for hosting applications with high IO workload requirements
  without having to configure sharding to spread the applications' workload across
  servers.

> **Note:** Not all Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions support extended storage. For details,
> see [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions).

Azure (Microsoft Azure) has volume modification cooldown periods that might limit how
frequently you can make storage changes.

> **Important:** Azure (Microsoft Azure) restricts disk IOPS/throughput adjustments to a maximum of four changes
> within a 24-hour period for Pv2 disks. After this limit is reached, MongoDB
> replaces the virtual machine (VM) to bypass this limitation.

The following statements describe the options you have in the Atlas UI
for IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) and storage management on Azure (Microsoft Azure) clusters.

- Initially, when you choose an Azure (Microsoft Azure) **Low CPU** or
  **General** cluster, Atlas assigns the default storage
  size and IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) based on the tier you choose.

- Depending on the specific characteristics of the storage volumes used
  in your region and whether the cluster is in the
  [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
  you can do one of the following actions:

  - If your region doesn't [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
    you can change storage capacity only in preset amounts and can't change
    the IOPS using the slider.

  - If your region [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
    you can use the **Storage** slider to increase the default storage
    capacity within the same tier. Atlas also increases IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) to correspond
    to the increased storage size.

- If, after you increase the storage volume's size, the increased number
  of IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) still isn't sufficient, and the cluster's tier is in a
  [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
  you can further extend the number of IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) using the **IOPS** slider,
  without changing the cluster tier.

- In addition, you can also expand the total cluster storage beyond
  the default limits for the cluster's tier in
  [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions).
  You enable extended storage sizes in the [project settings](project-settings).

> **Note:** When increasing IOPS for a cluster deployed on Azure, values are limited to the cluster tier's maximum IOPS.

The following sections describe how to increase the storage size and IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second).

- [increase](change-storage-azure-steps)

- [Extend IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on Azure (Microsoft Azure)](extend-iops-azure-steps)

sections.heading: Change Data Volume Size on Azure (Microsoft Azure)
sections.body:
To change the server's data volume size:

- [View Cluster Details](view-cluster-details).

- In the **Cluster tier** > **Storage** section, you can
  do one of the following actions, depending on the region:

  - If your region [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
    move the slider to the right and choose your preferred disk size.

    The text box to the right of the slider changes dynamically and indicates
    the new disk size. Atlas also increases IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) to correspond to
    the increased storage size.

  - If your region doesn't support extended storage, you can increase the
    disk capacity in preset amounts. In this case, Atlas doesn't increase
    IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second).

  For storage capacity increases, Atlas increases the storage capacity
  in place without copying data or performing an [initial sync](manual/core/replica-set-sync).

  > **Note:** If you change to another region or to a multi-region cluster,
  > the specific storage volume capabilities or extended storage might
  > be unavailable. The Atlas UI notifies you that your IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) will
  > be reduced. This change might impact the application's performance,
  > the speed of restoring data from a backup, or copying backups from
  > these regions to other regions. To preserve the same performance,
  > continue using the same region. To learn more, see
  > [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions).

  Azure (Microsoft Azure) doesn't allow decreasing the storage capacity in place. To reduce
  the storage capacity of a cluster on Azure (Microsoft Azure), Atlas provisions new
  volumes, and then syncs data from the old to the new volumes using a
  logical or file copy based initial sync. Before you apply your storage
  capacity reductions, the **Review Changes** page notifies you that
  Atlas triggers a rolling restart of your cluster when you make this change.

  An initial sync copies data across the network and rebuilds all indexes. During
  this time, you can still access your cluster, but each node that Azure (Microsoft Azure)
  modifies remains unavailable until the node's initial sync completes. For large
  clusters, initial syncs for each node might take several hours to complete.

To expand the total cluster storage beyond the limits for the cluster
tier, you can enable extended storage in the [project settings](project-settings)
for [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions).

Azure (Microsoft Azure) supports extended storage with the following limitations:

- `M40`: up to 4TB

- `M50`/`M60`: up to 8TB

- `M80+`: up to 14TB

> **Note:** Extended storage is available only for `M40+` clusters. To change your
> cluster's tier, see [Modify a Cluster](modify-cluster).

If you enable extended storage, this slows down initial syncs and cross-project
snapshot restores, and can reduce high availability. Enabling extended
storage is a temporary solution for clusters that might require additional
storage capacity in the future. We recommend that you enable [sharding](manual/sharding)
for long-term expanded storage capacity.

sections.heading: Extend IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on Azure (Microsoft Azure)
sections.body:
The following procedure describes how to obtain extended IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second), **on top of**
the number of IOPS that you receive when you [increase](change-storage-azure-steps)
the storage size within the same cluster tier.

You can change the number of IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) for `M40+` Atlas clusters
for [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions).

To extend the number of IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second):

- [View Cluster Details](view-cluster-details).

- In the **Cluster tier**, change to the `M40` or higher cluster tier.
  The IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) slider displays in the **IOPS** section.

- If you haven't already, [increase](change-storage-azure-steps)
  the storage size.

- If your application requires additional IOPS on top of increased IOPS
  that you received by increasing storage, you can extend IOPS further.
  Move the **IOPS** slider to the right and choose your preferred
  number of IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second). Extending IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) affects [cost](instance-storage-speed).

sections.heading: Change Storage Capacity on Google Cloud
sections.body:
Google Cloud supports extended storage with the following limitations:

- `M40`: up to 4TB

- `M50`/`M60`: up to 8TB

- `M80+`: up to 14TB

> **Note:** Extended storage is available only for `M40+` clusters. To change your
> cluster's tier, see [Modify a Cluster](modify-cluster).

Unlike AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud doesn't have volume modification cooldown periods.
Therefore, storage changes can be made immediately without waiting for
previous modifications to complete.

When you increase a cluster's storage capacity, Atlas modifies the
size of the servers' data volumes in a rolling manner with zero
downtime. Atlas increases the storage capacity in place without
copying data or performing an [initial sync](manual/core/replica-set-sync).

However, Google Cloud doesn't allow in-place storage capacity reduction.
If you reduce the storage capacity of a cluster, Atlas
provisions new volumes, and then syncs data from the old to the new
volumes using a logical or file copy based initial sync.

If you enable extended storage, this slows down initial syncs and cross-project
snapshot restores, and can reduce high availability. Enabling extended
storage is a temporary solution for clusters that might require additional
storage capacity in the future. We recommend that you enable [sharding](manual/sharding)
for long-term expanded storage capacity.

Before you apply your storage capacity reduction, the
**Review Changes** page notifies you that Atlas triggers a
rolling restart of your cluster when you make this change.

Atlas has disk capacity limits on single replica sets, scaling up to
4 TB for higher cluster tiers. To expand the total cluster storage
beyond default limits, you can enable extended storage in
the [project settings](project-settings). To accommodate further
scaling in the future, enable [Configure a sharded cluster](cluster-option-sharding)
for long-term expansion.

sections.heading: Change Storage Capacity for Multi-Cloud Provider Clusters
sections.body:
Atlas selects the lowest common denominator across the three
cloud providers. This ensures consistency across the multi-cloud
deployment.

You can't adjust the IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) for your multi-cloud cluster.

sections.heading: IOPS (AWS Only)
sections.body: AWS-backed `M30+` clusters offer the option to provision IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second).

sections.heading: Provisioned IOPS vs Standard IOPS in AWS
sections.body:
Provisioned IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) let you customize the maximum IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) rate for your
cluster. They also:

- Deliver their configured IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) rate more consistently when compared
  to standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second).

- Lower your cluster's p90 latency (measurement of the
  server's response time). 90 percent of server requests have responses
  faster than the p90 latency value, so a lower p90 latency value means
  a generally faster response time.

To learn more about the merits of using provisioned vs standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second),
see [Amazon EBS-optimized instances](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ebs-optimized.html). See the following
summary:

- General Purpose SSD volumes are designed to deliver their baseline
  performance 99% of the time.

- Provisioned IOPS SSD volumes are designed to deliver their provisioned
  performance 99.9% of the time.

> **Note:** Changes to IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) provisioning affects characteristics, performance,
> and [cost](instance-storage-speed). When you select
> **Provision IOPS**, the storage changes from
> **General Purpose SSD** volumes to **Provisioned IOPS SSD** volumes.

sections.heading: Configure the IOPS Rate
sections.body:
If you do not select the Provision IOPS option when you create your `M30+` tier
cluster, the cluster uses standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second). The default standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second)
rate changes as the cluster's storage capacity changes. If you want to
provision an exact IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) value, enable provisioning.

The minimum standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) for `M30+` tier clusters is 3000.
The standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) value remains at 3000 unless you set the cluster storage size
to 1TB or more. If the storage for your `M30+` cluster meets or exceeds 1TB,
Atlas increases the standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) rate using an IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) to a storage
ratio of 3:1, up to `16k` IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) for AWS (Amazon Web Services) network storage volumes.

Local NVMe (non-volatile memory express) SSD (Solid State Disk) class clusters must use standard IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second).

Provisioned IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) is supported only for `M30+` tier AWS (Amazon Web Services)
clusters. To provision IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) for your `M30+` tier AWS (Amazon Web Services)
cluster, select Provision IOPS and either:

- Specify the exact IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) rate in the text box, *or*

- Move the slide bar until the text box displays your preferred IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second)
  rate.

> **Note:** The available provisioned IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) range for a cluster relates to
> disk [storage capacity](storage-capacity-ui). Changing your
> cluster's storage capacity changes the range of available
> provisioned IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second).

sections.heading: Minimum Disk Capacity to RAM Ratios
sections.body:
Atlas enforces the following minimum ratios for given cluster
tiers. This keeps cluster performance consistent with large datasets.

Instance sizes **M10** to **M40** have a ratio of disk capacity to
system memory of 60:1. Instance sizes greater than **M40** have a ratio
of 120:1.

> **Example:** To support 3 TB (or 3,072 GB) of disk capacity, select a cluster
> tier with a minimum of 32 GB of RAM. This would be **M50** or
> greater.

Atlas has disk capacity limits on single replica sets, scaling up to
4 TB for higher cluster tiers. To expand the total cluster storage
beyond default limits, you can enable extended storage in
the [project settings](project-settings). To accommodate further
scaling in the future, enable [Configure a sharded cluster](cluster-option-sharding)
for long-term expansion.

> **See Also:** To learn more about the default resources and available
> configuration options for each cloud service provider, see:
>
> - [cluster configuration options](amazon-aws-configuration-options)
>
> - [cluster configuration options](google-gcp-configuration-options)
>
> - [cluster configuration options](microsoft-azure-configuration-options).

sections.heading: Auto-Scale Cluster Tier and Storage Capacity
sections.body:
Atlas cluster tier reactive auto-scaling is available for all dedicated
cluster tiers under the General
and the Low-CPU cluster classes.

For new clusters, Atlas automatically enables cluster tier
auto-scaling and storage auto-scaling.

Use **Auto-scale** options to configure your cluster to
automatically scale your cluster tier, storage capacity, or both in
response to cluster usage.

> **Important:** During a migration, if you restore a snapshot with a larger size
> than the storage capacity of the destination cluster, the cluster
> does not automatically scale.

You can [opt out](opt-out-autoscaling) of cluster tier and
storage auto-scaling. To learn more, see
[Cluster tier scaling](howitworks-scale-cluster-tier) and
[storage auto-scaling](howitworks-scale-cluster-storage).

> **See Also:** 
>
> - [Cluster Auto-Scaling](cluster-autoscaling)
>
> - [Connection Limits and Cluster Tier](connection-limits)
